title: Update operator and developer guidance for the redesigned protocol
plan_sections: Server-owned transaction variables vs client-managed inputs
goal: Produce operator-facing and developer-facing guidance that explains how the 0.6.0 resumable transaction protocol works, how to reason about active and no-active state, how to interpret canonical fields and artifacts, and how to avoid reintroducing identity, resume, or compatibility drift in future work.
why_this_ticket: The 0.6.0 redesign changes the mental model of the system. Even if the runtime implementation is correct, future work can easily drift if maintainers, operators, or downstream contributors continue using pre-0.6.0 assumptions such as ticket-derived transaction identity, status-driven dispatch, sentinel no-active handling, or heuristic recovery. This ticket turns the plan into operational guidance that protects the redesign after implementation.
scope: Create or update documentation aimed at operators who need to understand the lifecycle of active work, explicit terminal completion, resumability, and failure handling.
Create or update documentation aimed at developers who need to modify transaction, persistence, resume, verification, commit, or compatibility code paths.
Explain the canonical distinction between planning identity and runtime transaction identity.
Explain the minimal canonical continuation contract and the role of top-level next_action.
Explain the canonical no-active baseline and why it is structural rather than sentinel-based.
Explain the role of materialized state, event history, and issuance metadata in deterministic resume.
Explain bounded historical compatibility and explicit malformed-persistence failure behavior.
Explain how to reason about terminal completion, post-terminal normalization, and idempotent continuation.
Provide maintenance guidance that helps future contributors choose the correct plan sections to consult before changing runtime behavior.
out_of_scope: Adding new workflow semantics not present in the plan.
Rewriting all historical internal documentation unrelated to 0.6.0.
Replacing planning document formats used by clients.
inputs: Outputs from p0-t01 through p3-t04 as available
Any implementation notes produced by earlier tickets
Current operator- or developer-facing docs that describe runtime behavior
expected_outputs: A concise operator guide for understanding active work, resume behavior, terminal completion, and failure modes under 0.6.0.
A concise developer guide for modifying code without violating the canonical identity model, continuation contract, event model, or compatibility boundary.
A maintenance checklist mapping common code-change scenarios to the relevant plan sections that must be reviewed first.
A short glossary of protocol terms to prevent concept drift in future tickets and pull requests.
documentation_requirements: Explain that ticket_id is planning identity and tx_id is canonical runtime identity.
Explain that the minimal canonical continuation contract is composed of active_tx, status, next_action, verify_state, commit_state, semantic_summary, and last_applied_seq.
Explain that status classifies lifecycle state while next_action dispatches continuation.
Explain that no-active state is represented structurally through active_tx: null and status: null rather than through sentinel identifiers.
Explain that materialized state is the canonical resume entrypoint and event history is the canonical rebuild and audit source.
Explain that commit is non-terminal and done is the only successful terminal outcome.
Explain that post-terminal work must normalize into the canonical no-active baseline without leaving resumable active work exposed.
Explain that historical compatibility is bounded and must not weaken new-write semantics.
Explain that malformed, ambiguous, or non-deterministically replayable persistence must fail explicitly.
Explain that optional phase-style, step-style, session-derived, or guidance-only metadata must not control deterministic continuation.

needs: How to tell whether work is active, resumable, terminal, or absent
How to interpret next_action and top-level status
How to understand verify, commit, and explicit terminal completion
How to understand no-active baseline behavior
How to recognize explicit failure versus bounded compatibility handling
Which plan sections are authoritative for identity, persistence, resume, and compatibility changes
Which fields are canonical versus derived or optional
How to avoid reintroducing heuristic recovery or sentinel state
How to preserve exact active-transaction continuation
How to reason about event history, materialized state, and issuance metadata when modifying code

acceptance_criteria: The resulting documentation accurately reflects the 0.6.0 plan and the implemented runtime behavior.
The documentation clearly distinguishes planning identity from runtime transaction identity.
The documentation clearly explains status versus next_action responsibilities.
The documentation clearly explains the canonical no-active baseline and the absence of sentinel transaction identifiers in normal flow.
The documentation clearly explains bounded compatibility versus explicit malformed-persistence failure.
The documentation gives future maintainers an explicit mapping from common change types to the relevant plan sections that must be consulted.
The documentation reduces the risk of reintroducing identity drift, heuristic recovery, status-driven dispatch, or sentinel no-active handling.
review_checkpoints: Confirm that the documentation never describes ticket_id as canonical runtime identity.
Confirm that the documentation never implies commit is terminal success.
Confirm that the documentation never treats status as the primary dispatcher when next_action is present.
Confirm that the documentation never describes no-active state using sentinel identifiers.
Confirm that the documentation never softens malformed-persistence failure into heuristic fallback.
maintenance_checklist_requirements: For identity-related changes, direct readers to Canonical Runtime Model, Server-owned transaction variables vs client-managed inputs, and Identity Model.
For resume-related changes, direct readers to Canonical Invariants, Resume Model, Canonical State Machine, and Materialized state.
For persistence-related changes, direct readers to Persistence Model, Canonical Ordering and Durability, and Compatibility Policy.
For event-related changes, direct readers to Canonical Checkpoints and Events and Transaction Semantics.
For terminal-handling changes, direct readers to Terminal completion is explicit, Post-terminal materialization, and Idempotent continuation requirements.
notes: This ticket should translate the final 0.6.0 protocol into durable maintenance guidance rather than restating the plan verbatim.
If implementation details differ from earlier assumptions, the guidance must follow the final implemented behavior only where it remains consistent with the plan.
The documentation should be concise enough to be used operationally but explicit enough to prevent concept drift in future work.
The output of this ticket should become the default onboarding reference for anyone modifying resumable transaction behavior after 0.6.0.
